presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: My arm went weak but it got better
I couldn't speak properly for a few minutes
Sudden facial drooping that resolved quickly
Brief episode of slurred speech and weakness

history_data.hpi: Patient experienced sudden onset left-sided facial drooping, arm weakness, and slurred speech approximately 2.5 hours ago while at work. Symptoms peaked within seconds and completely resolved within 18 minutes. By the time patient arrived at hospital (approximately 2.5 hours after onset), neurological examination was completely normal. Patient describes it as a frightening episode but feels completely well now.
patient_quotes: My arm suddenly went numb and weak.
I couldn't get the words out of my mouth.
The left side of my face felt strange.
Everything lasted maybe 15–20 minutes then went away completely.
I'm terrified this means I'm going to have a stroke.
pmh_pool: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 8 years ago
Previous TIA — 3 years ago, transient left arm weakness
Smoking history — quit 10 years ago, 30 pack-year history
Coronary artery disease — had MI 5 years ago

social_history.smoking: Former smoker — quit 10 years ago, 30 pack-year history
social_history.alcohol: Occasional alcohol use — 2–3 drinks per week

cardiovascular: Heart rate irregular — atrial fibrillation noted
neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII intact — no facial asymmetry
Motor strength 5/5 throughout — no pronator drift

investigations.Blood Glucose: 6.8 mmol/L — normal range. Hypoglycaemia excluded as cause of transient neurological symptoms.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, Creatinine 94 µmol/L, Urea 6.5 mmol/L, eGFR 68 — all normal. Baseline renal function acceptable for future imaging with contrast.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 8.5 × 10⁹/L, Hb 13.8 g/dL, Platelets 258 × 10⁹/L — normal. No thrombocytosis or haematologic abnormality identified.
investigations.Lipid Profile: Total cholesterol 6.5 mmol/L, LDL 4.3 mmol/L (elevated), HDL 0.9 mmol/L (low), Triglycerides 2.2 mmol/L (elevated) — abnormal lipid profile consistent with cardiovascular risk. Suboptimal control despite atorvastatin.
investigations.HbA1c: 68 mmol/mol (8.4%) — elevated, indicating suboptimal diabetes control.
investigations.Coagulation Screen (PT/APPT/INR): PT 12 seconds, APPT 29 seconds, INR 1.0 — normal. Patient on apixaban (DOAC) for atrial fibrillation; coagulation parameters normal.
investigations.ECG: Atrial fibrillation with ventricular response 82 bpm. No acute ST changes. No evidence of acute MI. Confirms chronic atrial fibrillation — important embolic stroke risk factor.
investigations.Thyroid Function (TSH/T4): TSH 2.3 mIU/L, Free T4 14 pmol/L — normal. Hyperthyroidism excluded as cause of atrial fibrillation.
investigations.CT Head (Non-contrast): No acute intracranial haemorrhage. No acute infarction visible. Normal brain parenchyma. No mass or midline shift. Imaging does not explain transient symptoms but rules out acute stroke.

patient_rules.emotional_state: concerned but reassured by normal examination

essential_questions: exact time when symptoms fully resolved
description of symptoms — focal neurological deficit?
arm or leg weakness — which side, how severe?
speech difficulty — expressive, receptive, or dysarthria?
vision loss — monocular or field defect?
essential_examinations: comprehensive neurological examination — looking for residual deficits
vital signs — especially blood pressure and pulse regularity
fundoscopy — assess optic disc and retinal vessels
critical_actions: recognize TIA from history of COMPLETE resolution despite focal neurological symptoms
distinguish TIA from stroke — key difference is COMPLETE resolution
ABCD2 risk stratification: Age ≥60, Blood pressure elevated, Clinical features (unilateral weakness/speech), Duration >60 min, Diabetes

assess stroke risk — this patient is HIGH RISK: Age 68, BP elevated, unilateral weakness + speech = 4 points, Duration 18 min = 0 points, Diabetes = 1 point = ABCD2 score 5–6 (HIGH RISK, 8% stroke risk at 2 days)
arrange urgent vascular imaging to exclude carotid stenosis (though not available in current investigations)
optimize risk factor control — blood pressure, lipids, glucose, smoking cessation
rapid neurology referral for specialist assessment
red_flags: Sudden onset focal neurological deficit (even if transient)
Multiple risk factors: age, hypertension, diabetes, atrial fibrillation
Previous TIA — indicates very high risk of future stroke
Suboptimal control of risk factors despite medications
Speech difficulty + unilateral weakness — suggests vascular event not other cause
acceptable_differentials: Acute ischemic stroke — but symptoms completely resolved (so TIA instead)
Migraine with aura — but no headache, different symptom pattern
Focal seizure with Todd's paralysis — but no convulsions, no post-ictal period
Bell's palsy — would be isolated facial weakness, not combined with arm weakness
Vertebral artery dissection — would have headache or neck pain
management_actions: URGENT TIA clinic referral — ideally same day or next day
Continue apixaban 5mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation stroke prevention
Continue aspirin 81mg daily for secondary prevention (note: patient already on dual therapy with apixaban, so antiplatelet may be reviewed)
Blood pressure target: <140/90 mmHg — consider uptitrating amlodipine or adding additional agent
Lipid management: intensify atorvastatin — consider increasing to 80mg daily given LDL not at goal; target LDL <1.8 mmol/L for very high-risk patients
Diabetes management: intensify glycaemic control — target HbA1c <53 mmol/mol; increase metformin or add GLP-1 agonist
Reinforce smoking cessation counselling — patient quit 10 years ago but emphasize importance
Urgent carotid imaging (carotid ultrasound or CT/MR angiography — NOT available in this investigation set but critical in real practice) — rule out significant stenosis requiring endarterectomy
Urgent ECG (already done) — confirm atrial fibrillation
Lifestyle modifications: diet, exercise, stress reduction
Driver safety counselling — may need to notify driving authority depending on jurisdiction
Patient education on TIA warning signs and stroke prevention
Arrange follow-up appointment with neurology and primary care within 1 week
important_investigations: Carotid imaging — NOT available in frontend but CRITICAL in real practice
critical_history_questions: Exactly what time did symptoms START?
Exactly what time were symptoms COMPLETELY GONE?
What was the first symptom you noticed?
Describe any weakness — which arm? which leg? How severe?
Any facial drooping — which side?
Any speech difficulty — could you form words? Could you understand others?
Any vision loss — one eye or field loss?
Any numbness or tingling?
Any vertigo or balance problems?
Did ANY symptom persist after the main episode resolved?
Have you had similar episodes before? When?
Do you have atrial fibrillation? How long diagnosed?
Are you on anticoagulants? Which one? Dose?
Are you on antiplatelet therapy? Which drug?
How is your blood pressure normally? Any recent readings?
How is your blood sugar control? Any recent HbA1c or glucose readings?
When was your last cholesterol check? What were the results?
Are you a current or former smoker?
Have you had a previous stroke or TIA?
Any family history of stroke?
